title: SEGMENT 16 — Novel checkpoints: LAG-3, TIGIT, TIM-3, and beyond

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Novel checkpoints: LAG-3, TIGIT, TIM-3, and beyond
Beyond CTLA-4 and PD-1, the immune system has more brakes.
Many more.
The field has, for the last decade, been hunting for the next checkpoint molecule that, when blocked, will unleash anti-tumor immunity in a new way.
Some hunts have succeeded.
Many have failed.
Let me walk through the major ones.
LAG-3.
Lymphocyte-activation gene 3.
LAG-3 was identified in the 1990s as a marker on exhausted T cells, and it functions, broadly, as another brake.
It binds MHC class II and other ligands, and when engaged it dampens T-cell activation.
The clinical development of anti-LAG-3 antibodies took a long time and was punctuated by frustration.
Multiple early trials of LAG-3 inhibitors as monotherapy showed only modest activity.
The breakthrough came with relatlimab, an anti-LAG-3 antibody from BMS, tested in combination with nivolumab in the RELATIVITY-047 trial.
The combination, known as Opdualag, was approved by the FDA in March 2022 for advanced melanoma.
It produced improved progression-free survival over nivo alone, with toxicity that was — as I mentioned earlier — closer to nivo monotherapy than to ipi-nivo.
It is a gentler, less efficacious alternative to ipi-nivo for melanoma.
Multiple other LAG-3 inhibitors are in development, but Opdualag is the first.
LAG-3, in some sense, is the third Nobel-worthy checkpoint after CTLA-4 and PD-1.
Whether the eventual prizes get given is for the Karolinska Institute to decide, but the molecule is now firmly in the clinic.
TIGIT.
T-cell immunoreceptor with immunoglobulin and ITIM domains.
TIGIT is another inhibitory receptor on T cells and NK cells.
For several years, anti-TIGIT antibodies were the most-hyped new checkpoint in oncology, with multiple companies racing to develop them.
The hype crashed in 2024.
Roche's Phase 3 trial of its anti-TIGIT antibody tiragolumab plus atezolizumab in lung cancer failed to meet its overall survival endpoint.
Multiple other TIGIT programs reported disappointing data around the same time.
The TIGIT story became, briefly, a cautionary tale about expecting too much from preclinical data and early-phase results.
Some TIGIT programs continue — there are reasonable arguments that specific patient subsets, or specific antibody-engineering choices, might still produce wins.
But the field, as of 2025, is much more skeptical than it was three years ago.
The TIGIT story is a useful reminder that not every checkpoint inhibitor works.
The cell-biology principle — block a brake, release T cells — is necessary but not sufficient.
Whether a specific brake matters in a specific clinical context depends on the brake, the cancer, the patient's immune state, and the combination.
Generalization from CTLA-4 and PD-1 has limits.
TIM-3.
T-cell immunoglobulin and mucin-domain containing-3.
Another exhaustion marker, another inhibitory receptor.
Multiple anti-TIM-3 antibodies are in clinical development.
Results so far have been modest.
The field is still waiting to see whether TIM-3 will produce a clinical winner.
Other checkpoints.
The list keeps growing — VISTA, BTLA, B7-H3, BTN3A1, and others.
Many are targets of antibodies or fusion proteins in early-phase trials.
Some will succeed; most will fail.

The pattern is that, just as CTLA-4 and PD-1 emerged from a much larger pool of candidate molecules, the next clinically important checkpoint will likely emerge from the current crowded pipeline through a combination of biology, luck, and design.

The broader strategic question is whether we should be hunting for more checkpoints at all, or whether the remaining problems in checkpoint inhibition are better addressed through combinations, smarter biomarkers, and getting T cells into cold tumors in the first place.
Different parts of the field bet on different answers.
The truth is probably that all of these strategies will, eventually, contribute.
For now, LAG-3 is in the clinic with Opdualag.
TIGIT is in retreat.
TIM-3 and others are coming up the pipeline.
The brakes are many, and the field is, brake by brake, working through them.
